title: Centaurea castellanoides
intro: Centaurea castellanoides, also known as Star-thistle or Centaurea, is a perennial herb native to the Mediterranean region. Traditionally, Centaurea castellanoides has been used to treat a range of health issues, including inflammation, fever, and digestive problems, due to its anti-inflammatory, antiseptic, and antioxidant properties. The bioactive constituents of this plant include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal effects. Centaurea castellanoides is commonly used in herbal preparations such as teas, infusions, and tinctures, although it's worth noting that its use should be in consultation with a healthcare professional due to potential toxicity and interactions.
taxonomy: Centaurea castellanoides is classified as a species of flowering plant in the daisy family, Asteraceae. It belongs to the genus Centaurea, which comprises a large number of thistle-like species. The plant's classification is further detailed under the family Asteraceae, subfamily Carduoideae, and tribe Cardueae. This classification is based on the plant's botanical characteristics, such as its leaf structure and flower arrangement. As a member of the Asteraceae family, Centaurea castellanoides shares similarities with other plants in this family.
uses: Centaurea castellanoides has been traditionally used in folk medicine, with various reports suggesting its potential benefits in treating gastrointestinal issues. The plant's extracts may have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which could contribute to its medicinal uses. Some studies indicate that Centaurea castellanoides may have antimicrobial and antifungal effects. The plant's roots and leaves are sometimes used to treat digestive problems and skin conditions. Further research is needed to confirm the efficacy and safety of using Centaurea castellanoides for medicinal purposes.

constituents: Centaurea castellanoides, a species of thistle, has been found to contain a variety of active constituents. These constituents include flavonoids, such as kaempferol and quercetin, which are known for their antioxidant properties. Additionally, the plant has been found to contain phenolic acids, including chlorogenic acid and caffeic acid, which have been shown to have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The plant also contains sesquiterpene lactones, which have been found to have anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory effects. Further research is needed to fully understand the active constituents of Centaurea castellanoides.
